stage.name: README, User Manual, Design Docs: which source answers which question
stage.description: A source-grounded OpenHW SlideKit microcourse that teaches learners how to choose the right official CVA6 source before making technical claims.
stage.courseTemplateName: Professional Technical Deep Dive - Evidence-Grounded SlideKit

content.title: README, User Manual, Design Docs: which source answers which question
content.subtitle: A practical document router for reading CVA6 without mixing repository setup, IP scope, requirements, and microarchitecture detail.
content.thesis: High-quality CVA6 learning starts by choosing the right source for the right kind of question.

sourceAnchors.title: CVA6 GitHub README positioning and repository map
sourceAnchors.locator: github-openhwgroup-cva6/README.md CVA6 RISC-V CPU, Quick setup, Directory Structure
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: The README gives the repository-level CVA6 positioning, quick setup, top-level directory roles, and APU versus core separation.

sourceAnchors.title: CVA6 documentation organization
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: The documentation overview separates the User Guide, Requirements Specification, Design Document, configuration-specific design documents, and CVA6 APU documentation.

sourceAnchors.title: CVA6 User Manual audience and limits
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: The User Manual targets software programmers, integrators, architects, hardware designers, and verification engineers, but says internal mechanisms are covered by separate design documentation.

sourceAnchors.title: CV32A60X Design Document objective
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: The CV32A60X design document provides enough information for RTL modification and RTL verification, and is not aimed at software-only instruction or register lookup.

content.speakerNotes: Before we go deeper into architecture, we need a document router. CVA6 has a README, a User Manual, a Requirements Specification, and configuration-specific design documents. They are not interchangeable. A strong reader first asks, what kind of question am I answering, then opens the source that is built to answer that question. That habit prevents a lot of confident but wrong technical explanations.

actions.text: Before we go deeper into architecture, we need a document router. CVA6 has a README, a User Manual, a Requirements Specification, and configuration-specific design documents. They are not interchangeable. A strong reader first asks, what kind of question am I answering, then opens the source that is built to answer that question. That habit prevents a lot of confident but wrong technical explanations.

content.title: Do not let one document answer every question
content.subtitle: Most CVA6 mistakes are not from reading too little. They come from asking one source to do a job it was not designed to do.

items.title: Repository question
items.body: Where is the code, how do I build it, and which directory owns core versus APU material?

items.title: Integration question
items.body: What is CVA6, which configurations exist, and what belongs inside the documented IP scope?

items.title: Contract question
items.body: What shall the IP support, and what belongs to verification or product-level requirements?

items.title: Microarchitecture question
items.body: How does a specific configuration structure modules, stages, interfaces, and registers?

sourceAnchors.title: CVA6 GitHub README positioning and repository map
sourceAnchors.locator: github-openhwgroup-cva6/README.md CVA6 RISC-V CPU, Quick setup, Directory Structure
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: The README gives the repository-level CVA6 positioning, quick setup, top-level directory roles, and APU versus core separation.

sourceAnchors.title: CVA6 documentation organization
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: The documentation overview separates the User Guide, Requirements Specification, Design Document, configuration-specific design documents, and CVA6 APU documentation.

sourceAnchors.title: CVA6 requirements as what-level contract
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: The requirements specification is organized as requirements that apply to the IP scope and supports the feature and verification contract.

sourceAnchors.title: CV32A60X Design Document objective
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: The CV32A60X design document provides enough information for RTL modification and RTL verification, and is not aimed at software-only instruction or register lookup.

content.speakerNotes: This lesson is a small workflow, but it is the workflow that makes later depth possible. If the question is about cloning, simulation entry points, or directory ownership, start with the README. If the question is about IP scope or who the manual is for, use the User Manual. If the question is about required behavior and verification scope, use the requirements. If it asks how a module works, use a design document for the exact configuration.

actions.text: This lesson is a small workflow, but it is the workflow that makes later depth possible. If the question is about cloning, simulation entry points, or directory ownership, start with the README. If the question is about IP scope or who the manual is for, use the User Manual. If the question is about required behavior and verification scope, use the requirements. If it asks how a module works, use a design document for the exact configuration.

content.title: Four official sources, four different jobs
content.subtitle: Treat these as complementary lenses, not competing summaries.

cards.label: README
cards.title: Repo and first contact
cards.body: Best for project positioning, quick setup, repository directories, tutorials, and core versus APU folder ownership.

cards.label: User Manual
cards.title: Integration and scope
cards.body: Best for target audience, configuration family, IP boundary, interfaces, programmer-visible behavior, and references.

cards.label: Requirements
cards.title: What must be true
cards.body: Best for feature scope, product-level commitments, verification targets, optional features, and out-of-scope items.

cards.label: Design Docs
cards.title: How one variant works
cards.body: Best for CV32A60X or CV32A65X internals, module hierarchy, pipeline behavior, ports, parameters, and registers.

content.takeaway: The source choice is part of the technical answer.

sourceAnchors.title: CVA6 GitHub README positioning and repository map
sourceAnchors.locator: github-openhwgroup-cva6/README.md CVA6 RISC-V CPU, Quick setup, Directory Structure
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: The README gives the repository-level CVA6 positioning, quick setup, top-level directory roles, and APU versus core separation.

sourceAnchors.title: CVA6 User Manual audience and limits
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: The User Manual targets software programmers, integrators, architects, hardware designers, and verification engineers, but says internal mechanisms are covered by separate design documentation.

sourceAnchors.title: CVA6 User Manual configuration family
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: The User Manual describes CVA6 as a family of cores with more than 50 parameters and lists verified configurations such as CV32A60X and CV32A60AX.

sourceAnchors.title: CVA6 requirements as what-level contract
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: The requirements specification is organized as requirements that apply to the IP scope and supports the feature and verification contract.

sourceAnchors.title: CV32A60X Design Document objective
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: The CV32A60X design document provides enough information for RTL modification and RTL verification, and is not aimed at software-only instruction or register lookup.

sourceAnchors.title: CV32A60X Design Document framework
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: The design document framework splits the processor into modules and describes module features, functionality, architecture, hierarchy, and registers when applicable.

content.speakerNotes: Here is the map I want you to keep. README is first contact. User Manual is integration and scope. Requirements is the what-level contract. Design documentation is the how-level explanation for one configuration. Notice the last phrase: one configuration. CVA6 is parameterized, so a design claim from CV32A60X should not automatically become a universal claim about every CVA6-based core.

actions.text: Here is the map I want you to keep. README is first contact. User Manual is integration and scope. Requirements is the what-level contract. Design documentation is the how-level explanation for one configuration. Notice the last phrase: one configuration. CVA6 is parameterized, so a design claim from CV32A60X should not automatically become a universal claim about every CVA6-based core.

scenes.title: README: start here, but do not stop here

content.title: Use README for orientation and repo mechanics
content.subtitle: The GitHub README is excellent for entering the project, but it is not the final authority for every microarchitecture detail.

columns.label: Good uses
columns.title: Ask README when...
bullets: You need the short project positioning.
You need quick setup, submodules, toolchain, or simulation entry points.
You need the top-level directory split between core, APU, docs, util, vendor, and verification.

columns.label: Limits
columns.title: Do not overuse README for...
bullets: Precise requirement coverage or verification scope.
Claims that depend on a particular configuration.

content.takeaway: README is the lobby, not the whole building.

sourceAnchors.title: CVA6 GitHub README positioning and repository map
sourceAnchors.locator: github-openhwgroup-cva6/README.md CVA6 RISC-V CPU, Quick setup, Directory Structure
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: The README gives the repository-level CVA6 positioning, quick setup, top-level directory roles, and APU versus core separation.

content.speakerNotes: The README is the project lobby. It tells you CVA6 is a six-stage, single-issue, in-order CPU, gives setup steps, and explains the repository structure. That is very useful. But a README is intentionally compact. When you need exact behavior for one configuration, or a module-level explanation, use the manuals. Otherwise, you may accidentally turn a convenient overview into an overconfident technical claim.

actions.text: The README is the project lobby. It tells you CVA6 is a six-stage, single-issue, in-order CPU, gives setup steps, and explains the repository structure. That is very useful. But a README is intentionally compact. When you need exact behavior for one configuration, or a module-level explanation, use the manuals. Otherwise, you may accidentally turn a convenient overview into an overconfident technical claim.

scenes.title: User Manual: integration, scope, and family view

content.title: Use the User Manual for what integrators need to know
content.subtitle: It explains CVA6 as a configurable family and marks the boundary between user-facing reference and internal design material.

columns.label: Best for
columns.title: Integration questions
bullets: What is CVA6 at a high level?
Which verified configurations are being addressed?
What belongs inside or outside the IP scope?

columns.label: Important warning
columns.title: Manual boundary
bullets: The manual says internal mechanisms are not its main target.
A separate design document digs into core microarchitecture.
So use it to locate the question, then move deeper only when needed.

content.takeaway: User Manual answers the boundary and user-facing layer before the internal layer.

sourceAnchors.title: CVA6 User Manual audience and limits
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: The User Manual targets software programmers, integrators, architects, hardware designers, and verification engineers, but says internal mechanisms are covered by separate design documentation.

sourceAnchors.title: CVA6 User Manual configuration family
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: The User Manual describes CVA6 as a family of cores with more than 50 parameters and lists verified configurations such as CV32A60X and CV32A60AX.

content.speakerNotes: The User Manual is where the project becomes less vague. It names the target audiences, explains CVA6 as a configurable family, and lists configurations such as CV32A60X, CV32A60AX, and CV64A6 MMU. It also warns us that internal mechanisms live elsewhere. That warning is gold: it tells us when to stop summarizing and start reading the design document.

actions.text: The User Manual is where the project becomes less vague. It names the target audiences, explains CVA6 as a configurable family, and lists configurations such as CV32A60X, CV32A60AX, and CV64A6 MMU. It also warns us that internal mechanisms live elsewhere. That warning is gold: it tells us when to stop summarizing and start reading the design document.

content.title: Use requirements when the question says shall, support, scope, or verify
content.subtitle: Requirements are not a tutorial. They are a contract for behavior, scope, optionality, and verification intent.

steps.title: Scope
steps.body: Identify what the IP includes and what is explicitly outside the IP.

steps.title: Feature
steps.body: Read whether a feature is required, optional, or only a desired goal.

steps.title: Configuration
steps.body: Check which release or viable configuration the requirement applies to.

steps.title: Verification
steps.body: Translate the requirement into what a DUT or test plan may need to cover.

content.takeaway: Requirements tell us what must be true before we ask how it is implemented.

sourceAnchors.title: CVA6 requirements as what-level contract
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: The requirements specification is organized as requirements that apply to the IP scope and supports the feature and verification contract.

content.speakerNotes: The Requirements Specification is where wording becomes contractual. It is organized around requirements that apply to the IP scope. This is where you ask, what shall the hardware support, which optional feature is parameter-controlled, and what is outside the measurement boundary. It is not the best place to learn the pipeline story. It is the place to keep the pipeline story honest.

actions.text: The Requirements Specification is where wording becomes contractual. It is organized around requirements that apply to the IP scope. This is where you ask, what shall the hardware support, which optional feature is parameter-controlled, and what is outside the measurement boundary. It is not the best place to learn the pipeline story. It is the place to keep the pipeline story honest.

content.title: Use design documents for module-level technical depth
content.subtitle: The CV32A60X design document explicitly targets RTL modification and RTL verification, so it is the right source for internal architecture teaching.

cards.label: Objective
cards.title: For designers and verificators
cards.body: The CV32A60X document aims to provide enough information for RTL modification and RTL verification.

cards.label: Framework
cards.title: Module-by-module reading
cards.body: The processor is split into modules. Chapters describe features, functionality, architecture and module hierarchy, and registers when relevant.

cards.label: Variant discipline
cards.title: Keep the configuration name visible
cards.body: A CV32A60X detail is a CV32A60X detail unless another source supports generalizing it across CVA6.

content.takeaway: Design docs are where the classroom should become technical, but also configuration-specific.

sourceAnchors.title: CV32A60X Design Document objective
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: The CV32A60X design document provides enough information for RTL modification and RTL verification, and is not aimed at software-only instruction or register lookup.

sourceAnchors.title: CV32A60X Design Document framework
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: The design document framework splits the processor into modules and describes module features, functionality, architecture, hierarchy, and registers when applicable.

sourceAnchors.title: CVA6 Read the Docs generated manuals
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: The docs README explains that configuration-specific manuals include tailored RISC-V ISA material and design documentation, with some inputs generated from RTL.

content.speakerNotes: Now we reach the source that should carry most deep technical lessons. The CV32A60X design document says its purpose is to help designers modify RTL and verification engineers verify it. It also describes a module framework: description, functionality, architecture and module hierarchy, and registers where needed. That is exactly the structure we should exploit for future microcourses.

actions.text: Now we reach the source that should carry most deep technical lessons. The CV32A60X design document says its purpose is to help designers modify RTL and verification engineers verify it. It also describes a module framework: description, functionality, architecture and module hierarchy, and registers where needed. That is exactly the structure we should exploit for future microcourses.

questions.question: You want to know which top-level folder contains CVA6 core-only source files. Which source should you open first?
questions.analysis: The README is the fastest official source for repository mechanics and top-level directory ownership.

questions.question: You need to explain what is inside the documented CVA6 IP boundary before teaching integration. Which source family is most appropriate?

options.label: User Manual introduction and IP scope section

questions.analysis: The User Manual introduction and scope section are designed for boundary and integration framing.

questions.question: A slide says CV32A60X has a specific module behavior. What should the source anchor normally point to?

options.label: A community discussion with no source link

questions.analysis: Variant-specific internal behavior should be grounded in the matching design document, not generalized from a short overview.

questions.question: Which pairings are professionally safe?

options.label: README -> quick setup and repository structure
Requirements -> what the IP shall support and verify
User Manual -> every internal pipeline implementation detail
Design Doc -> module hierarchy and RTL-oriented explanations

questions.analysis: A, B, and D match source intent. The User Manual helps with scope and integration but explicitly points internal mechanisms to separate design documentation.

sourceAnchors.title: CVA6 GitHub README positioning and repository map
sourceAnchors.locator: github-openhwgroup-cva6/README.md CVA6 RISC-V CPU, Quick setup, Directory Structure
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: The README gives the repository-level CVA6 positioning, quick setup, top-level directory roles, and APU versus core separation.

sourceAnchors.title: CVA6 User Manual audience and limits
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: The User Manual targets software programmers, integrators, architects, hardware designers, and verification engineers, but says internal mechanisms are covered by separate design documentation.

sourceAnchors.title: CVA6 requirements as what-level contract
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: The requirements specification is organized as requirements that apply to the IP scope and supports the feature and verification contract.

sourceAnchors.title: CV32A60X Design Document objective
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: The CV32A60X design document provides enough information for RTL modification and RTL verification, and is not aimed at software-only instruction or register lookup.

content.speakerNotes: This checkpoint is not trivia. It tests whether you can route a question before answering it. If the topic is setup or folder ownership, start with README. If it is scope and integration, use the User Manual. If it is shall-level behavior, use Requirements. If it is how a module works in CV32A60X, use the design document. This routing skill is what turns a document pile into a usable engineering map.

actions.text: This checkpoint is not trivia. It tests whether you can route a question before answering it. If the topic is setup or folder ownership, start with README. If it is scope and integration, use the User Manual. If it is shall-level behavior, use Requirements. If it is how a module works in CV32A60X, use the design document. This routing skill is what turns a document pile into a usable engineering map.

scenes.title: Summary: turn sources into a reading system

content.title: A good CVA6 lesson starts with a source decision
content.subtitle: This is the quality-control rule we will reuse for every future microcourse.
takeaways: README is for entry, setup, and repository orientation.
User Manual is for scope, integration, configurations, and user-facing reference.
Requirements define what the IP must support and how verification scope is framed.
Design documents carry module-level, configuration-specific technical depth.
content.nextStep: Next lesson: reading the official scope diagram without inventing unsupported details.
content.closing: Route the question first. Then make the technical claim.

sourceAnchors.claimSupported: The README gives the repository-level CVA6 positioning, quick setup, top-level directory roles, and APU versus core separation.

sourceAnchors.title: CVA6 documentation organization
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: The documentation overview separates the User Guide, Requirements Specification, Design Document, configuration-specific design documents, and CVA6 APU documentation.

sourceAnchors.title: CVA6 User Manual audience and limits
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: The User Manual targets software programmers, integrators, architects, hardware designers, and verification engineers, but says internal mechanisms are covered by separate design documentation.

sourceAnchors.title: CVA6 requirements as what-level contract
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: The requirements specification is organized as requirements that apply to the IP scope and supports the feature and verification contract.

sourceAnchors.title: CV32A60X Design Document objective
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: The CV32A60X design document provides enough information for RTL modification and RTL verification, and is not aimed at software-only instruction or register lookup.

content.speakerNotes: The takeaway is simple, and a little unforgiving in the best way. Do not let one source answer every question. README starts the journey. User Manual frames scope and integration. Requirements define what must be true. Design documents explain how a specific configuration works. From now on, every high-quality CVA6 microcourse should show which layer it is using before it makes a claim.
